fields.description:
# S2 ÔÇö Auth framework + Resource-Server wiring + static-bearer verifier

## Why
This story delivers the authentication admission core ÔÇö the provider-agnostic seam every
verifier plugs into ÔÇö plus the first (and simplest, dependency-free) verifier so the whole path
is exercisable locally: the static bearer token (the "PAT" half of the confirmed floor). It
turns S1's HTTP transport into an *authenticated* endpoint.

## What
- Extend the existing **flat** `McpConfig` dataclass (today `readonly`/`allow_llm`/
  `allow_jira_sync`) and the **flat** `_SECTIONS['mcp']` keyÔćĺcoercer dict in
  `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` ÔÇö the current one-level `coerce_sparse` architecture, which
  handles only flat `sectionÔćĺscalar-key` mappings (NO nested sub-tables, NO list-of-tables) ÔÇö with
  additional **flat scalar** auth keys: `auth_enabled` (bool, default false), `auth_strategies`
  (comma-separated string coerced to an ordered list), `auth_issuer_url` (str),
  `auth_resource_server_url` (str), `auth_required_scopes` (comma-separated string Ôćĺ list), and
  `auth_static_tokens_file` (str path to a secrets file the static verifier parses itself). Each is
  exactly one dataclass field + one flat coercer entry, auto-deriving the matching `REBAR_MCP_*`
  env var; update `MCP_ENV_VARS`/`server.json`. **No nested `[mcp.auth]` sub-table and no
  variable-length structure is placed in the config schema** (see the static-token format below).
- New module `src/rebar/_mcp_auth.py`: a `CompositeTokenVerifier` with an explicit,
  non-contradictory contract ÔÇö it tries each configured verifier in order; a verifier that returns
  an `AccessToken` wins; a verifier that returns `None` means "not my token" and the composite
  tries the next; a verifier that **raises** is an internal error ÔÇö the composite logs it and
  **immediately fails closed (denies)**, never swallowing the exception to `None` and never falling
  through to another verifier; if no verifier accepts, the request is denied (401). Plus a
  configÔćĺverifier **factory** (builds the active verifiers from `auth_strategies`); the
  **`StaticBearerVerifier`** (SHA-256 hash-at-rest, `hmac.compare_digest` constant-time compare,
  per-token `client_id`/`scopes`, mandatory audience/scope handling). All implement the official
  SDK `mcp.server.auth.provider.TokenVerifier` protocol (`verify_token(token)->AccessToken|None`).
- Wire it in `build_server`: when `auth_enabled`, pass `token_verifier=CompositeTokenVerifier(ÔÇŽ)`
  and `auth=AuthSettings(issuer_url=ÔÇŽ, resource_server_url=ÔÇŽ, required_scopes=ÔÇŽ)` to `FastMCP`, so
  the SDK serves the RFC 9728 PRM document and emits 401 + `WWW-Authenticate`. Resolve S1's
  unauthenticated-HTTP guard against the real `auth_enabled` value.
- **Mandatory audience validation:** the SDK does NOT validate `aud`/resource; the composite/base
  enforces it (equivalent to always-on `--oauth-strict`, RFC 8707) ÔÇö a token whose audience is not
  this resource server is rejected.

**Static-token secrets format (parsed by the verifier, NOT the TOML coercer).** The per-token
records live in the JSON/TOML file named by `auth_static_tokens_file`, which the
`StaticBearerVerifier` reads and parses at load ÔÇö deliberately OUTSIDE the flat config coercer
(which handles only one-level scalar keys, so it cannot represent a variable-length list of
multi-field records). Each record supplies `client_id`, `scopes` (list), and the token secret as
EXACTLY ONE of: `token_env` ÔÇö the NAME of an environment variable holding the plaintext token
(preferred; nothing secret in the file itself), OR `token_sha256` ÔÇö a precomputed lowercase SHA-256
hex digest. At load the verifier resolves the plaintext from `token_env` and stores only its SHA-256
(never retaining plaintext), or uses `token_sha256` directly; a record embedding a plaintext token
literal, or missing both fields, is rejected at startup (fail-closed).

## Approach & rationale
Model the framework on the SDK's own `TokenVerifier`/`AuthSettings` seam (the blessed extension
point the SDK's `simple-auth` example uses), and keep the config surface **flat** to fit rebar's
existing `coerce_sparse` architecture ÔÇö adding flat scalar keys alongside the existing flat
`readonly`/`allow_llm`/`allow_jira_sync` fields, exactly as that pattern already works, rather than
introducing nested config the coercer cannot handle. Variable-length/secret-bearing structure (the
per-token records) is pushed into a verifier-parsed file so the config schema stays flat. A composite
verifier (rather than a single selected strategy) lets a deployment accept, e.g., static PAT for CI
*and* OIDC JWTs for humans simultaneously ÔÇö the GitHub model ÔÇö without bespoke branching. Static
bearer first because it needs no external IdP, so it makes the end-to-end 401/200/403 path
verifiable locally.

## Scope
The auth framework (composite + factory + flat config + PRM/401 wiring + mandatory audience check)
and the **static** verifier only. JWKS/JWT (S3), introspection (S4), trusted-proxy (S5), custom (S6)
are separate stories that register additional verifiers on this seam. A subset of the epic.
Depends on S1.

## Security
Boundary-crossing (authenticated network endpoint). Requirements enforced here: constant-time
token comparison; only a SHA-256 hash of each static token is held in memory (no plaintext
retained; the plaintext is never written in config ÔÇö it comes from `token_env` or is pre-hashed);
tokens are never logged; audience validated on every request; **fail-closed** ÔÇö a verifier that
raises is logged and denies the request immediately (no fall-through), misconfigured auth refuses to
start, and no verifier accepting yields 401; insufficient scope Ôćĺ 403. The static-token secrets file
is documented as dev/internal-grade in S7.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] The flat `mcp.auth_enabled`/`auth_strategies`/`auth_issuer_url`/`auth_resource_server_url`/
      `auth_required_scopes`/`auth_static_tokens_file` keys exist as `McpConfig` fields + flat
      `_SECTIONS['mcp']` coercer entries, with matching `REBAR_MCP_*` env vars in `MCP_ENV_VARS`/
      `server.json` (observable oracle: the manifest drift gate `scripts/check_server_manifest.py`
      passes and a config round-trip test reads each key from TOML and from its env var).
- [ ] Integration test (tier: integration ÔÇö an in-process ASGI HTTP client, e.g. httpx
      `ASGITransport`, driving the server's Streamable-HTTP ASGI app within its lifespan/session
      context; the concrete `mcp` SDK entrypoint is resolved against the pinned `mcp>=1.28,<2` at
      implementation time) asserts, with static auth enabled: an unauthenticated request returns
      **401** and a `WWW-Authenticate` header carrying the RFC 9728 `resource_metadata` URL
      (observable oracle: status + header value).
- [ ] Integration test asserts the PRM document is served at
      `/.well-known/oauth-protected-resource/ÔÇŽ` with an `authorization_servers` field
      (observable oracle: HTTP 200 + JSON field present).
- [ ] Integration test asserts a valid static token Ôćĺ **200** and an unknown/invalid token Ôćĺ 401;
      a token whose principal lacks a `required_scope` Ôćĺ **403** (negative controls included).
- [ ] Unit test asserts `StaticBearerVerifier` holds no plaintext token (only its hash) and uses a
      constant-time compare (observable oracle: the in-memory store contains the SHA-256 digest,
      not the token), that a wrong-audience token is rejected (returns None), and that a secrets-file
      record embedding a plaintext token literal (or omitting both `token_env` and `token_sha256`)
      is rejected at startup (observable oracle: startup raises with a clear message).
- [ ] Unit test asserts the composite's error contract: a verifier returning `None` falls through
      to the next, a verifier returning an `AccessToken` wins, and a verifier that **raises** causes
      an immediate deny (no fall-through, no swallow-to-None) ÔÇö observable oracle: the request is
      denied and the exception is surfaced/logged, with a negative control where the raising verifier
      is not consulted after an earlier accept.
- [ ] With `mcp.auth_enabled=false`, an integration test asserts no auth middleware is installed:
      an HTTP request with **no** token receives a normal MCP response (observable oracle: not 401 ÔÇö
      identical to S1's transport behavior), and the stdio suite `tests/interfaces/facades/test_mcp.py`
      still passes unchanged.
- [ ] `make lint && make typecheck && make test` pass.

## Compatibility
Additive; auth off by default. The config change only ADDS flat scalar keys to the existing flat
`McpConfig`/`_SECTIONS['mcp']` ÔÇö no change to the coercer architecture, to stdio, or to the existing
tools. Depends on S1's transport.
